summary: Jeff Atwood explores whether LCD HDTVs could serve as cost-effective alternatives to expensive large computer monitors. He notes that 30-inch monitors are disproportionately expensive compared to their smaller counterparts, costing twice as much for only 1.7x the pixels. Citing PC Gamer's editor, he argues that ultra-high resolutions like 2560x1600 can actually be counterproductive since text becomes harder to read. The post concludes that 1080p HDTVs offer a compelling value proposition: larger screens at significantly lower prices, thanks to economies of scale in the TV market.
key_points: Large computer monitors have diminishing returns on price-to-pixel ratio, with 30-inch displays costing 2x more for only 1.7x the pixels of 23-inch models
Higher resolution isn't always better - 2560x1600 on 30-inch monitors can make text harder to read than 1920x1200 on 24-inch displays
Operating systems still struggle with resolution scaling, leaving us trapped in a bitmapped world rather than vector-based rendering
LCD HDTVs benefit from economies of scale that make them substantially cheaper than equivalent-sized computer monitors
The 37-inch Westinghouse HDTV at $1,099 offers a larger display than Apple's $1,999 30-inch Cinema Display
money_quotes: 30" is nice, but you're paying 2x the price of the 23" for 1.7x the number of pixels.
Counter-intuitively, text and fonts are larger on 1920x1200 24-inchers than they are on 2560x1600 30-inchers.
You'll have to sit closer to a 30-inch monitor than you will a 24-inch monitor in order to comfortably read text.
We still live in a bitmapped world.
LCD HDTVs look like an outstanding deal for large monitor enthusiasts.
They're larger, and due to economies of scale, should always be substantially cheaper than computer monitors, too.
key_insight: The pursuit of ever-higher monitor resolutions may be misguided when our software can't scale properly, making cheaper HDTVs with 'lower' resolutions potentially more usable than expensive high-DPI monitors.
